# Transcrição: 20-Blackstone Valley Sniper

- **Fonte:** 20-Blackstone Valley Sniper
- **Duração:** 07:22
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:01]

I open my eyes and the room is dark. I'm panting, I'm coming out of a nightmare. I roll over to get comfort from my wife, but my wife is a terrible human being. She doesn't abide anyone touching her while she is sleeping. So as I roll to hold her, she pushes me back into the darkness. And so I lie by myself, and I think about the nightmare I just came out of, which isn't really a nightmare, but like this reoccurring memory that I've been living with for 30 years, this thing that plagues me every night from childhood. It's 1986 and I'm 15 years old. I'm sitting at the dinner table. I'm eating grub. Grub is a mixture of baked beans and hamburger and anything else that my mom can throw into the pot. And it really doesn't matter what it is. It could be like a cantaloupe or a Snickers bar. Like the grub just assimilates all of the food that she throws in and it just becomes this brown slurry that sits on the stove all week long. And we eat grub every night because we're poor. Like we have been always poor, but now we're really poor. We're like grub poor. And I don't really mind grub. It's really not that bad, except I can't invite friends over anymore because you can't invite your friends for grub. Like my last name is Dick's. I understand the power of a name and how like a bad name can really ruin a lot of things. And so I'm stuck eating this grub. And so I'm eating it. And my mother declares that she has an announcement, which makes us all freeze because like we don't talk as a family at dinner time. We're like monks. We come and we eat our grub and we disperse to our corners of the house as quickly as possible. We're like private contractors just living in this place and waiting to get out of it as quickly as possible. But she says she has an announcement. She says we're going to play a new game, which makes us all wonder what the hell she's talking about. We don't play games in our house. We used to play games when my mother and father were together when I was a little boy. My father was the greatest game player of all time. He had the best games. He had who can stay in the dryer the longest. He had a game called All the Underwear where you would just put all your underwear on. And he had a game where my brother and I would sit on the stove and he would turn the burners on and it was who can stay on the burner the longest. It sounds terrible, but no game has ever compared to that game like nothing in the world has compared to the joy of who can stay on the burner the longest. My mother says the game we're going to play is crawl through the living room at night. I opened my mouth to say like that is not a game. And she says, and it's important because we put the Christmas tree up and I don't want people to not be able to see the tree when you're walking through the living room. I want to tell my mother like this is stupid. Like this makes no sense. But then she says, if we do it till the end of the month

## [03:01 - 06:01]

she's going to give us $10 each, which is like a fortune. Like I can buy in my mind at least a million Star Wars figures with $10. Like none of us can believe it. And so my brothers and sisters and I, we jump into this game. We're going to crawl through the living room at night no matter what. And it's not easy because the only way to get to the upstairs is through the living room. So we're crawling back and forth constantly. My sister is the first one to fail. Three days in she starts walking through the living room and the mother says, Kelly down. And Kelly drops like a sack of potatoes. And I'm thinking, ma, like this is not jump off the burner. This is just go crawl through the living room. What is your problem? But I look at her face and I can see there's like something. She's got like wild eyes. I just decide I'm going to leave it. A couple of weeks later, I forget to crawl through the living room and she yells at me, Matt, get your ass down. And again, I'm thinking like, what the hell is wrong with my mother? Two days later, I find the newspaper in the trash can and I take it out and I know what the hell is wrong. On the front page of the one socket call, there's a story about the Blackstone Valley Sniper, a man who is shooting into the windows of homes at night in the area that I live. Seven people have been shot by this man. No one has been killed yet, but it's on the front page of the paper and I found it buried in the trash. And I realized my mom is trying to protect us, but even greater than that, she's trying to protect us from the knowledge that this is even happening. And so I take that newspaper and I shove it as far into the trash as possible because I don't want my brothers and sisters to know. And we play that game until the end of December and she gives us our $10. And we play it in January and we play it in February. And all of us get our money. And the game ends in February. And there's no explanation why, but I'm never able to walk through that living room again at night without terror in my heart. And so I'm lying in my bed thinking about this dream that comes back to me all the time. And for the first time in my life, I wonder like what the hell really was going on in 1986? So I go downstairs to the computer and I Google Blackstone Valley Sniper and it comes up right away. It wasn't one guy, it was two. They shot at 11 homes. They never killed anyone, but they got caught and they got put in prison for life. And I'm staring at this, just realizing what my mom did for us. Like that grub doesn't seem so bad anymore. Like I never thought she was very good. And now I'm starting to think that maybe she was holding things together in a way I didn't see. And so I open up my blog and I write about my mother who has passed away by now.

## [06:01 - 07:22]

And for the first time in my life, I write positively about my mom as a parent. I write about how she took care of us in a way I could not see at the time. And I post it and less than half an hour later, I get an email from a woman who tells me she's the girlfriend of one of the Blackstone Valley Snipers. She must have a Google Alert set up on his name. And she's angry with me because I've called him a monster in what I've written and she says, how dare you? She says he's been paroled and he's trying to change his life. And he deserves a chance. You shouldn't be writing about him as a monster. And she demands that I take down what I've written. And I won't, I can't. Like I've written about my mom and I'm not going to undo what I've done. And I do believe in redemption. Like I believe that if that man has done his time, he can have his chance. But I believe in monsters too. And I've been living with him as a monster in my life for a very, very long time. And now I believe in my mom. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you.
